parts.text: At dawn she found old Sero, the blind ferryman who hadn't crossed water in a decade but still sat at the dock speaking truth to anyone brave enough to listen. She told him everything. He was quiet a long time. Then he said something that struck her like a thrown stone. The dead man didn't test you Maren. Your own hunger did. And hunger wearing the mask of fairness is the most dangerous kind because you feed it and call it justice. She wanted to argue. She wanted to say she deserved something.

The reasonable voice spoke again, soft as a lover, fierce as a blade: Take the village road, open it tonight, bury half under the fig tree where he lies, keep the rest—no one will ever know, and the sea keeps every secret it’s ever been told. She took one step down the warm familiar path, then another, legs moving before permission was granted. Halfway home the weight shifted inside the iron, clinking like bones laughing. She stopped. Turned.

In the morning she walked back down the ridge lighter by a fortune and heavier by a stone she could never sell without selling herself. Years later, when the cliff cottage finally crumbled into the sea, fishermen still spoke of the quiet woman who mended every net for free and kept a single blue sapphire sewn inside her coat, close enough to feel its cold pulse whenever she was tempted to believe her own excuses again.
